title: SVM
text: Support Vector MachineIntroductionSupport Vector Machine is a very famous classification algorithm ( can be used in regression challenges as well ) in Machine Learning, which occupied the dominant position of classification algorithms before Deep Learning. Even today, there are still many cases where SVM will be used. This article will explain SVM in detail, and in the end I will show the MATLAB codes for implementing different SVMs, which can help you to have a deeper understanding on how SVM works. What is SVMSVM is a kind of supervised machine learning algorithm, which usually used for two-classes classification. There are 6 key parts in SVM: Margins Primal Problem Lagrange Dual Problem Soft Margin Kernel Before we go into details, let’s start with figuring out what exactly SVM is doing. Let’s consider a scenario, ChewChew needs to apply for a loan from the bank to buy a house. The bank needs to decide whether to approve ChewChew’s application. Usually, the bank decided by age-income, and the bank had the following historical data: What should bank do if they want make the decision based on historical data? We may naturally want to draw a line to divide the historical data into two parts, if ChewChew drop in the upper half part, then approve his application, else rejection. The line we draw also called Hyperplane In geometry, a hyperplane is a subspace whose dimension is one less than that of its ambient space. If a space is 3-dimensional then its hyperplanes are the 2-dimensional planes, while if the space is 2-dimensional, its hyperplanes are the 1-dimensional lines. One Hyperplane can be express as $g(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}+b=0$ This Hyperplane will classify a given data \mathbf{x}_{i} : \begin{equation} \operatorname{sgn}\left[g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)\right]=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} {+1} & {\text { if }} & {g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)>0} \\ {-1} & {\text { if }} & {g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)
